summary: Justin Brennan coaches Brian Norton's AIOS community on writing Letters of Intent (LOI) for commercial multifamily acquisitions, including deal timelines, negotiation tactics, and underwriting discipline. He walks through a live LOI template, explains the offer submission process, and emphasizes how brokers use inflated assumptions to make deals appear better than they are.
takeaways: LOIs are non-binding, one-to-two page documents sent before purchase agreements; send them aggressively and frequently (aim for ~10-12 LOIs to land one deal)
Due diligence period is typically 30 days from PSA execution with full refundability of earnest money deposit during this window—use it to back out for any reason without disclosure
Economic loss assumption should be minimum 10% (vacancy + loss to lease + bad debt + concessions), not the 5% brokers quote; only high-coastal markets (Miami, San Diego, NYC) justify 5-7%

Cost of capital is the only legitimate way to outbid competitors; if all use traditional financing at same rates, revenue and expenses are identical, so higher bids require cheaper money or lower required returns

Closing timeline: 60 days standard + 30-day extension option; push back on 45-day closings unless you have a 30-day extension; extension deposits (few thousand dollars) are typically non-refundable after due diligence ends
Submit LOI via email only (no hard copies); immediately follow up with broker by phone/text using rapport-building language: 'I used your numbers, show me where I'm wrong'

Contact lenders and insurance brokers during offer phase for soft quotes to tighten underwriting; use vendor list in mentorship portal (Incorp for Wyoming LLC setup at $125/year, then get agent of service)

skill_candidates.change: Use 10% minimum economic loss in underwriting instead of accepting broker's inflated 5% assumption
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explicitly teaches Sandon to push back on brokers using basic math: 'you know darn well economic loss...is going to be north of 8 to 10 to 15%' and tells him to tell the broker 'I might as well just take a gun to my head' if he uses 5%, then provides exact script: 'John, listen. How long you've been doing this? 10 years. Great. You know darn well economic loss, not vacancy, economic loss on anything in this market is going to be north of 8 to 10 to 15%'

skill_candidates.change: Back pricing decisions into market rent comparisons instead of assuming tenant behavior; research comparable rents before making rent-raise decisions
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin teaches Relle on the 8-unit month-to-month lease scenario: 'You want to go online. You want to see what they're currently paying for rent...what the market is' and explains tenants will do the same: 'they're going to go on Zillow...apartments.com, and they're going to start looking for a replacement property'

skill_candidates.change: Send 10-20+ LOIs per deal sourced instead of perfecting each one; treat LOIs as conversation starters, not final offers
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin states: 'you may have to throw out 20 to get one, right? It's very, very possible...like a 10 or 12 to one...I've seen upwards of 20 to 1 on ratios. So, you know, just throw throw them out. It's it's non-binding. It means nothing. You're just trying to start the conversations'

skill_candidates.change: Call broker immediately after sending LOI to build rapport and start negotiations using broker's own numbers against them
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin coaches Sandon: 'you'll typically follow up with the broker and say sometimes almost immediately...You can say, Hey, John, I just sent you in an LOI...I used your numbers that you gave me, plugged it in, and I'm backing into some returns'

title: Weekly Coaching_08-14-25 | Justin Brennan
